DDoS攻撃がかつてない規模と速度で急増し、短時間で壊滅的な影響を与える時代に突入した。自動化と低コスト化で参入障壁が下がり、企業や分野を問わず被害が広がっている。  Gcoreの2025年後半レポートによれば、攻撃件数は2024年Q4の51万件から2025年Q4には130万件へと急増し、最大トラフィックも12Tbpsに達した。特にネットワーク層攻撃が全体の82％を占め、低コストで実行可能な点が拡大の要因とされる。75％の攻撃は1分未満と短時間化する一方、アプリケーション層では10分超の中長時間攻撃が64％に増加した。攻撃対象はテクノロジー分野が34％で最多となり、金融やゲームが続く。発信源はメキシコやブラジルなど中南米に集中し、ボットネットの影響が指摘される。攻撃は自動化が進み、アカウント乗っ取りやデータ取得など目的志向型へ変化している。 securityonline.info/gcore-ra…

A shocking eyewitness account has emerged from the UP College shooting incident, where a student lost his life inside the campus. Shivangi Singh, who was present at the scene, described how the incident unfolded and raised serious questions about campus security and response.  According to Shivangi, she had come to college as usual and was near the stairs after leaving her bag in the library. The victim, Surya Pratap Singh, was standing nearby with a friend when the first gunshot was fired. She said the attack happened so suddenly that Surya had no time to react. The accused allegedly fired immediately after arriving, without giving any warning.  Shivangi questioned how the attacker managed to enter the college premises with a weapon. She believes the gun was hidden and brought inside without detection, pointing to a major lapse in security. She explained that if the weapon had been visible earlier, the victim might have had a chance to escape.  Despite the chaos, Shivangi said she tried to help Surya, initially believing he had been shot in the chest and could still be saved. However, the attacker returned and fired again, this time fatally. She described how the entire sequence happened within seconds, leaving no time for anyone to intervene effectively.  She also expressed regret, saying that if more people had stepped forward, the outcome might have been different. According to her, only one teacher attempted to intervene, while others stayed back. She further alleged that even after the incident, there was a delay in providing immediate help, claiming that the college administration did not respond promptly. 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐇𝐢𝐝𝐝𝐞𝐧 𝐑𝐢𝐬𝐤 𝐢𝐧 𝐖𝐞𝐛𝟑: 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐎𝐫𝐚𝐜𝐥𝐞 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐛𝐥𝐞𝐦   The biggest threat to decentralized applications isn’t always volatility or hacks it’s bad data. This is what’s known as the “Oracle Problem.”  Blockchains are designed to be secure, trustless, and self-contained. But that strength is also a limitation.  They can’t naturally access real-world information like asset prices, sports results, or weather data.  So without a reliable bridge to external data, even the most advanced smart contract becomes ineffective—like a brain with no senses.  Bridging the “Truth Gap” in Web3   Imagine a DeFi protocol that relies on incorrect price data. Users could be liquidated unfairly.  Or a GameFi platform using manipulated randomness. The system becomes rigged.  This gap between blockchain logic and real-world truth is where oracles come in—and where WINkLink ($WIN) plays a critical role within the TRON ecosystem.  WINkLink connects smart contracts to real, verifiable data, ensuring that what happens on-chain reflects what’s actually happening off-chain.  How WINkLink Powers the TRON Ecosystem 🛠️  WINkLink is more than just a data provider it’s a decentralized “truth engine” that supports multiple layers of Web3 innovation:  • Reliable Price Feeds: It aggregates data from multiple exchanges to deliver accurate pricing for DeFi platforms, reducing risks like manipulation or single points of failure.  • Verifiable Randomness (VRF): Critical for blockchain gaming, ensuring outcomes are fair, transparent, and tamper-proof.  • Real-World Data Integration: From sports scores to weather updates and IoT signals, developers can build smarter, more dynamic dApps using real-world inputs.  Why WINkLink’s Bull Case is Strong in 2026   The growth of TRON is directly increasing the importance of its oracle layer. Here’s why:  1. Explosive Stablecoin Activity TRON is a dominant network for stablecoins, especially USDT. With billions flowing through its ecosystem, accurate and secure data feeds are no longer optional—they’re essential.  2. DeFi & RWA Expansion As Total Value Locked (TVL) grows and Real World Assets (RWA) enter the ecosystem, the demand for trustworthy data increases exponentially. WINkLink becomes the safeguard for all that value.  3. The “Oracle Standard” of TRON Just like every major blockchain needs a leading oracle solution, WINkLink has positioned itself as the go-to infrastructure layer for TRON developers—benefiting from every new dApp deployed.  4. Native Speed & Cost Advantage Because WINkLink is built within TRON, it offers faster updates and lower costs compared to many external oracle solutions—making it ideal for scalable, high-frequency applications.  The Deeper Reality: Data is Everything   In Web3, we often say “code is law.” But in reality, data is the evidence that law depends on. If a smart contract receives flawed or manipulated data, it will still execute—just incorrectly. WINkLink ensures that smart contracts operate on accurate, verified, and tamper-resistant data—bringing trust and reliability into every transaction.  Final Thoughts: Bet on the Infrastructure  As Web3 evolves from isolated systems into real-world financial infrastructure, oracle networks will become one of the most valuable layers in the stack.  WINkLink isn’t just supporting the TRON ecosystem—it’s enabling it to interact with reality.  Don’t just focus on the applications. Focus on what powers them.  Because in the long run, the real value often lies in the engine—and WINkLink is that engine for TRON’s future growth. @justinsuntron @WinkLink_Oracle

This is a great demo. The know-it-all contrarian replies make me cringe.  Here’s a quick explainer what’s going on AND why it’s much worse than it appears.   Copper Clad Aluminum (CCA) cables are copper cables with aluminum cores. They exist purely for the ~25% cost reduction. They almost always have cost-cutting insulation. Let’s touch on both topics, then combine into a unified nightmare :)  CCA vs solid copper: CCA has lots of negatives. The primary in this context is that it has ~60% more electrical resistance. This degrades high speed signals(2.5G/5G/10G), especially as you get near the limits of length. It also significantly increases how much the cable heat up. Let’s say you are doing PoE++ at 100W, across 100 meters, on a tiny 24 bundle pair. The CCA will produce ~150W of additional heat. Thats a small space heater! But it’s not evenly spread across the cable because…  CCA is also brittle, so any partial hairline cracks will become concentrated hot spots. CCA also doesn’t “bite” well when terminating, which creates another opportunity for hot spots. Worse: this gets worse over time due to aluminum oxidation.   And then we get to the insulation. Flame retardant and stiffeners cost money, so they are left out. That means you have insulation that gets very soft under much lower temperatures on a metal that gets much warmer. That the metal starts to squeeze together which will mess with the data signal, or worst case, allowing the start of a short (another hot spot). Keep in mind, these cables are often run in bundles (compounding the heat) and through hot locations like attics.   And if the insulation ever catches fire, it does NOT self extinguish. Yes, the cable itself could ignite, but ANYTHING starts a fire, you effectively have a fuse inside your walls now. That’s exactly what @TracketPacer is showing in this video.   Lots of commercial buildings have “fire walls” that stop the spread of fire between sections of the building. One of the few things allowed to pass through these fire walls is… data cables!   So to save 25% on your cabling, you are accepting a big degradation in data performance which will often lead to lots of debugging. It drops the voltage for PoE equipment… more debugging! Anytime the bundles are disturbed in any way… good chance of more debugging! And then you have the fire safety hazard and code violations that can leave you holding the bag for the consequences instead of the insurance.   STOP BUYING CHEAP CABLES!
